title: Built-in QC review, biostat sign-off & audit trail

changes.text: Outputs now follow the standard clinical review workflow: a second programmer completes a QC checklist (titles, populations, values, precision, footnotes, layout — with the anomaly scan attached), then the biostatistician signs off. Sign-off is only possible after a passed QC, and the old one-click approve is gone.

Every QC and sign-off is recorded with reviewer name and timestamp. Redoing or resetting a review archives the previous records instead of deleting them, and regenerating an output automatically voids its review.

New Audit Trail page per study: a tamper-evident, append-only record of every action — config edits (old → new values), data uploads (with SHA-256 checksums), TFL selection changes, generation runs, reviews, and downloads — with chain-integrity verification and CSV export.
Delivery packages now include the study's audit trail CSV alongside the manifest.

title: Reliability, more tables & a clearer interface

changes.text: Seven more tables can now be generated — analysis sets (14.1.1.3), protocol deviations (14.1.1.4), medical history (14.1.2.2), prior and concomitant medications (14.1.2.3 / 14.1.2.4), and the Grade ≥3 AE tables (14.3.1.3 / 14.3.1.4). They previously appeared in the list but failed when generated.
Failed generations now show the reason inline, with an expandable details view, instead of just a red badge.
Generating a batch no longer freezes the screen — jobs run in the background and progress updates live, scoped to the current run.
Table previews open and run automatically, show the real table name and number, and render in the same fixed-width font as the final RTF.
You can start a study before you have any data — choose “Skip — no data yet” to configure it and preview placeholder shells.
The AI assistant formats its answers, keeps your conversation when you navigate away, and tells you to add an API key instead of failing silently; AI errors now explain what went wrong.
Outputs: package only approved files when you want, remove an approval, and clearer confirmations throughout. Each study now shows its name with a link back to all studies.
Treatment-arm handling, the adverse-event figure, and study-completion rows no longer assume the reference study’s specifics, so other studies render correctly.
The app detects when another program is using its port and explains it, and shuts its background services down cleanly on exit.
Added this Updates page — a running history of every change across both the app and the underlying TLF automation library.
The app version now increases with each release and is shown here and on the Settings page; the installer filename matches it too.

The "Select TFLs" assistant is now data-aware — ask it to "select everything my data supports" — and shows a working indicator plus a plain-English summary of exactly what it changed (or why nothing changed).
In-app Settings: paste your Anthropic API key (saved locally and verified with a test call), see the app version, and check for updates — no more editing files.
Added an AI model dropdown so you can choose which Claude model the app uses; the options come live from your account.
Removed the native menu bar (File / Edit / View / Window).
Friendlier startup-failure screen with "Try again" and "Open logs" buttons.
One-command build (npm run build:app) that turns source changes into a fresh installer.
TLF Studio is now a downloadable Windows app — no Python, Node, or manual setup required.
One-click installer with Desktop and Start-menu shortcuts; the Python engine and web UI are bundled inside.
Automatic updates from GitHub Releases — installed apps update themselves in the background.
The AI assistant now streams its answers word-by-word.
Unified the Protocol / CRF / SAP upload experience and persisted SAP extraction results.
Polished the study overview dashboard and the configuration page.
Upload Protocol and CRF documents; AI extracts study metadata and category lists for you to review and edit.
SAP upload now accepts PDF or Word (.docx) files.
Create a study by selecting a whole data folder instead of picking files one at a time.
The table-chat assistant can query the underlying ADaM data (e.g. list subject IDs), not just the rendered table.
Figures now preview as images, and "Download RTF" generates the file on demand.
Table generation now respects human-reviewed CRF category lists (disposition-reason order, race / ethnicity, etc.).
Removed the slow RTF→PDF conversion step from generation.
Restored the CDISCPILOT01 ADaM data and added the randomization-by-country table.
Fixed table-preview headers and generation wiring.
Shell mode: generate empty CRO TFL shell templates (with "xx" placeholders) when no data is present.
Every table wired to match the CRO TFL template layout exactly.
Split the shell registry into one spec file per table / figure.
Support for the split shell registry and editing SAP configuration.
Aligned all outputs with the General Outputs Specification and added abbreviation footnotes.
Made column layouts study-agnostic and added synthetic test datasets (ABC201, pharmaverse).
Added ADRG, SDRG, and define.xml submission documents.
Audit fixes: demographics Sex/Race counts, headers and labels, AE coverage; auto-replace old dated outputs.
RTF→PDF conversion via Word, with a timeout to recover from COM hangs.
Condensed continuous-summary blocks to five rows and kept them together across page breaks.
CDISCPILOT01 TLF automation library — generates tables, listings, and figures to RTF from ADaM data.
TLF Studio web app — configure, generate, preview, and manage clinical TLF outputs.
